author: gante

body:
I'm curious how this difference doesn't break the tests 🤔 

Helpful script to compare the PT and TF version of this function:

```python
import torch
import tensorflow as tf
import numpy as np

# TF definitions and imports
import operator
from functools import reduce
from typing import Sequence
from transformers import shape_list

def _product(x: Sequence[int]) -> int:
    """Return product of elements of a list for python<=3.7."""
    prod = reduce(operator.mul, x, 1)
    return prod

def rotate_every_two_tf(x: tf.Tensor) -> tf.Tensor:
    rotate_half_tensor = tf.stack((x[:, :, :, 1::2], x[:, :, :, ::2]), axis=-1) # <-- the assertion fails without the change here
    new_shape = shape_list(rotate_half_tensor)[:-2] + [_product(shape_list(rotate_half_tensor)[-2:])]
    rotate_half_tensor = tf.reshape(rotate_half_tensor, new_shape)
    return rotate_half_tensor


# PT definitions and imports
def rotate_every_two_pt(x):
    x1 = x[:, :, :, ::2]
    x2 = x[:, :, :, 1::2]
    x = torch.stack((-x2, x1), axis=-1)
    return x.flatten(-2)  # in einsum notation: rearrange(x, '... d j -> ... (d j)')


# Comparison
var_pt = torch.rand(6, 6, 6, 6)
var_tf = tf.convert_to_tensor(var_pt.numpy())
assert np.allclose(var_pt.numpy(), var_tf.numpy())

rotated_pt = rotate_every_two_pt(var_pt)
rotated_tf = rotate_every_two_tf(var_tf)
assert rotated_pt.shape == rotated_tf.shape
assert np.allclose(rotated_pt.numpy(), rotated_tf.numpy())
```

body:
Okay, there are two paths to get past this error. In both cases, you can update these lines to
```python
def get_output_embeddings(self):
    return self.lm_head

def set_output_embeddings(self, new_embeddings):
    self.lm_head = new_embeddings
```

The two paths:
1. Replace the `lm_head`, which is a `Dense` layer, by a `TFWrappedEmbeddings` (see [here](https://github.com/huggingface/transformers/blob/b7018abf3ce34ed9e2d7dddb5fcf3a2af27a37f8/src/transformers/modeling_tf_utils.py#L2112)). There may be other associated changes that need to be done, see a model implementing it for an example (e.g. BART);
2. Skip the associated test 🙃  `TFWrappedEmbeddings` has TF 1.x behavior, and replacing it with modern code is on my to-do list. The functions that are associated with it, like `resize_token_embeddings`, are equally outdated, so the new version would work well with a `Dense` layer.

I'm leaning towards option 2., as solving this is pretty high on my priority list and 1. is painful work

In ML world I agree, because we know what are IDs of classes, and how to map 1 to its semantic meaning.

I don't think this is the case for someone outside ML. ID: 0 doesn't mean anything on its own so an array
[0, 2]
[2, 1]
Doesn't really convey any particular meaning on its own, you need to understand that ids are associated to classes and how to retrieve those.

Image bitmask exist outside the ML world and make all IDs handling uncessary. (You're only handling a `str` label which has meaning on its own)

body:
The ViT export with dynamic axes only works on `torch` v1.9 or the nightly release (i.e. the upcoming v1.11). In other words, it's not possible to export ViT with one batch size and then run inference with a different batch size in v1.10.x

I couldn't pin down exactly what the source of the problem was in `torch` v1.10.x (it seems like one of the ops in `torch.onnx` is buggy), but since v1.11 is about to be released my proposal is to assign a minimum `torch` version in `OnnxConfig`. By default this is v1.8, but can be overridden to accommodate these model-specific exceptions.

I'm not sure whether this approach of being selective about `torch` versions is allowed in `transformers` so please let me know what you think @sgugger @LysandreJik @michaelbenayoun :)

P.S. If we agree this approach makes sense, we should probably hold off on merging this PR until `torch` v1.11 is released

body:
Did a first pass, first of all great work, it's a huge step forward. Main things for me:

* it may be useful to have a separate `MaskFormerSegmentationModule` as `nn.Module`, that encapsulates the `class_predictor`, `mask_embedder`, `matcher` and `criterion`.
* a lot of code is written like `variable_name: annotation_type = ...` => not sure if we want to follow this paradigm of coding. It's a different way of coding for sure, and we're not using this type anywhere in the code base right now.
* as @sgugger already pointed out, there are a LOT of type hints, and while these can be useful sometimes, I feel like here they may hurt readability of the code a bit, especially since we already have docstrings that explain the type for each argument that a method expects, and a huge number of annotations are just `Tensor`.
* a lot of docstrings don't respect the [guide](https://github.com/huggingface/transformers/tree/master/docs) in Markdown, so please update them accordingly.

Once the CI is as green as possible and my comments are addressed, I'll do a second (and hopefully final) pass of the code.
author: NielsRogge

body:
```suggestion
*Modern approaches typically formulate semantic segmentation as a per-pixel classification task, while instance-level segmentation is handled with an alternative mask classification. Our key insight: mask classification is sufficiently general to solve both semantic- and instance-level segmentation tasks in a unified manner using the exact same model, loss, and training procedure. Following this observation, we propose MaskFormer, a simple mask classification model which predicts a set of binary masks, each associated with a single global class label prediction. Overall, the proposed mask classification-based method simplifies the landscape of effective approaches to semantic and panoptic segmentation tasks and shows excellent empirical results. In particular, we observe that MaskFormer outperforms per-pixel classification baselines when the number of classes is large. Our mask classification-based method outperforms both current state-of-the-art semantic (55.6 mIoU on ADE20K) and panoptic segmentation (52.7 PQ on COCO) models.*

```
author: NielsRogge

body:
Reviewed the modeling file. My main comments are:
- we should consistently use several arguments for labels/outputs instead of dictionaries
- we should remove the `PreTrainedModel` inheritance for building blocks and only leave it for the main models. Left comments on how to achieve this
- the `return_dict`/òutput_hidden_states` and `output_attentions` APIs should match the other models.
author: sgugger

body: We can't have distributed code in a modeling file as it's restricted to just one kind of distributed training. It won't necessarily work with PyTorch XLA or the variants that are developed around distributed training. As mentioned before, one solution could be to use Accelerate here.
author: sgugger

body: Nit: We usually use the classes for the losses.
author: sgugger

body:
As mentioned before, this only works on *some* distributed setup but not all (here CPU, one GPU and multi-GPU but not TPUs, distributed SageMaker etc...). So no, we can't keep it.

For distributed code we should use Accelerate as it provides a unified API that will work on all the distributed setups we want to support.
author: sgugger

body: I agree that this should be some generic class if we want to support more backbones, but it can also be left as is for now since we only support Detr as a decoder.
author: sgugger

body:
I don't think the `loss_dict` should be included actually. We only provide the loss for the model to work inside the `Trainer`, but if the user wants to modify the loss computation, they should do it themselves from the other outputs returned.

So `loss` should go first (necessary for compatibility with the Trainer when a user uses `return_dict=False`) and `loss_dict` should be removed.

body:
It's already a very good start.

I think we can make this better though by preventing the `tokenizer` from seeing `sampling_rate` and `stride` ever (which are concepts which it shouldn't have to deal with IMO).

Lots of comments/NITs.

If you want, I could take a stab to make a PR over you PR to better explain what I am talking about (like the state machine or the `stride` `sampling_rate` thing)
author: Narsil

body: `Optional`, no ?
author: Narsil

body: Seems fine, but if it's always the same, instead of duplicating the code everywhere (and potentially  missing to add it in subsequent models, using it once in the `tokenizer` seems also viable)
author: Narsil

body:
I think it makes more sense to design this differently IMO.

If we use `output_offsets` (an  `int` being the offset in the `tokens` space).
It keeps the tokenizer responsible for what it's responsible, and NOT the `sampling_rate`. It feel very wrong to me  that a tokenizer should care about a `sampling_rate`.

Then getting the seconds should be really `start_offset *  stride / sampling_rate` and boom you get seconds.
Then we only need one argument to this function, and leave `sampling_rate` and `stride` responsability where they belong (the model, and the feature_extractor).
author: Narsil

body:
I don't think this is correct.

Since you already removed `PAD` tokens here then the indices will be wrong:

`A, A, PAD, PAD, B, C`
You'll output `{A, start: 0, end: 4}` if I am not mistaken where it should be `{A, start:0, end: 2}, {B, start: 4, end: 5}`,

No ?
author: Narsil

body:
It took me a while during review to understand what is meant here by stride.

IIUC, it's the reduction factor between `sample_space` to `token_space` (and is the product of `config.conv_stride`.

IMHO, other names could be better:
- `reduction_factor`
- `scaling_factor`
- `sample_to _token_ratio`

Or combinations of those words.

wdyt ?
author: Narsil

body:
I don't think we should attempt to do "word" boundaries directly here (maybe a helper function).

First we're output roughly twice the same data, then, word don't mean whitespace separated in many languages (like chinese, I even argue it's the case in German but it's not the point).

Taking only `subtitling` for instance as a use case, you don't really want "word" but you want "sentences", with a cap on length of text. You may even get sentence boundary, not looking at text, but looking at the padding length in seconds between spoken text (`token_timestamp[i + 1].stop - token_timestamp[i].start`, no idea if it works, but definitely seems like a sound strategy for subtitling I would like to attempt).

Making it *raw* by default is good, (pure timestamps per character). Making it easy to retrieve custom boundaries simple would be greater. Using your function as an option (in the docs maybe) seems better as opt-in.
author: Narsil

body: I don't think that's true. The `end_time` of a word, is not the `start_time` of a space.

Comment: State machines like this are always are to read/understand. It might very well deserve a few simple tests.

I also think we can make the code cleaner/better, but ti would still be hard to understand so I think we can keep this with a few tests (corner cases are double spaces, spaces at the boundaries and making sure we don´t missing the final word, which I think you do currently)
author: Narsil

body:
Regarding char vs word time stamps: 
The traditional forced alignment approaches usually require a second (external) tokenizer to split the predictions into words/lines/sentences and output the time spans for those specific units. How do you feel about including that option here, to support languages without explicit word boundaries? 

Alternatively, we could allow the user to supply the tokens directly to `_retrieve_tokens_with_time_stamps()` and support both external tokenization and forced alignment with ground-truth transcriptions!
author: anton-l

body: I agree here with @Narsil about moving the calculation to the tokenizer. For future non-w2v2 models (e.g. Conformer) the tokenizer will probably be different too, so this won't hurt the flexibility.
author: anton-l

body: Moving the entry point to the Processor is definitely a good idea, this makes the access to `stride` and `sampling_rate` less awkward than if it was completely inside the tokenizer. :slightly_smiling_face:

body: I understand your point, but disagree here because the tokenizer also outputs text as words - it has a specific `word_delimiter_token` and clearly puts a white space between words which holds true for almost all languages (except the few ones that are character based)

body: Should be correct as is - the "end_time" is the start time of the next space. Note that Wav2Vec2 was trained to finish always with a word delimiter boundary

Isn't the model trained to output [PAD] on silences ? 

If true, then I think we should exclude [PAD] tokens from the timings. (and so End != start of next)
If [PAD] strictly means "I am no sure" to the model then OK. 


My main concern is that the timings should exclude silences when they exist. Since your code doesn't seem to allow "holes" in the timings I think it cannot account for silences.

body:
I'm not sure if this statement is correct. BART is pre-trained with sentence-permutation and text-infilling objective. Where the input text is noised and is passed to the encoder and the decoder is tasked to generate the original document. See this figure from the paper.
![Screenshot 2022-02-21 at 12 16 06 PM](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/27137566/154945352-8d939035-2d1e-4693-9c6f-47695758bfac.png)

So for this task it's not possible to create `decoder_input_ids` by shifting `input_ids`.

This is only True for the sequence-classification task where we feed the same input to both encoder and decoder.
see this figure from the paper: 
![Screenshot 2022-02-21 at 12 16 24 PM](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/27137566/154945595-cc313308-2ece-4fb4-9048-e896cb4f91bd.png)

cc @patrickvonplaten

body:
This is a good question, and I should probably document it more clearly (including in the `to_tf_dataset` docs). `tf.data.Dataset` objects emit either a dict `features` or a tuple of two dicts `(features, labels)` when you iterate over them. The `columns` argument sets which columns of the input dataset will be used as keys in the `features` dict and the `label_cols` argument sets which columns of the input dataset will be used as keys in the `labels` dict. If `label_cols` is not set then the `tf.data.Dataset` will only emit a `features` dict.

The default behaviour of Keras is that it expects to get `(features, labels)` tuples as input. It does a forward pass with the `features` to get model outputs, and then passes the outputs and labels to the loss function specified in `compile()` to get a loss.

The problem with that, though, is that our models often have weird losses that are really painful for users to implement correctly - e.g. BERT has a token cross-entropy loss which should only be computed on tokens selected for masking, plus a next-sentence prediction loss. No user is ever going to correctly specify a Keras loss function for that, so we need to help them out.

The solution I used was to take advantage of the fact that our models compute a `loss` tensor during the forward pass if you pass the required labels as keys in the input dict. This is the standard way to train them with PyTorch - you just pass everything in the input dict and then do `outputs.loss.backward()`. In Keras, you can do the same thing - you just specify a "dummy" loss function that reads that loss value and outputs it directly as the loss.

So if the user `compile`s our models without a `loss` argument, we interpret that as them wanting to use the internal loss, and we quietly stick a dummy loss function on the `loss` output head of the model. If they're doing this, they have to include all the label columns as keys in the input dict (`compile()` throws a warning when you don't specify a loss to remind you to do this). If, on the other hand, the user specifies a loss when calling `compile()`, then we just use the standard Keras behaviour. In that case, it doesn't matter whether the label keys are in the input dict or not because we totally ignore the `loss` output head, but the user does have to pass a separate labels array or dict (usually by using the `label_cols` argument to `to_tf_dataset`) so that Keras can find them.
author: Rocketknight1

body: The fact that this was confusing to you 100% means it's confusing to all of our other users too, though - I do think that it's a neat interface once you're familiar with it, but it's not very Keras-idiomatic. We should probably think of places to document this so that it's clear to people, like the `to_tf_dataset` docs and possibly a `Getting started with Transformers and Tensorflow" doc as well.

Why would people want to resize the logits to the size of the `pixel_values` (which is typically something like 512x512 or 640x640 i.e. square)? All they care about is resize the logits to the size of the original image, both for evaluation + visualization.

body:
Yes but I wouldn't add this in the same spirit as why we don't add custom losses. If people want to use this model with a custom loss, they can easily use our model implementation to get the `logits`, then do whatever they want with it.

They don't need to re-implement the whole model for that.
author: NielsRogge

body: I agree with @NielsRogge , the only use case I can come up with is a custom loss, and usually, the interpolation is done in the loss directly. Moreover, if we give the ability to resize the logits we should also be sure the user can decide how the logits are interpolated ("bilinear" vs something else)
author: FrancescoSaverioZuppichini

body:
To expand my point, suppose people want to get the `logits` the same size as the `pixel_values` in order to compute a custom loss. 

Here's how they can achieve that without having to override the entire model:
```
import transformers import SegformerPreTrainedModel, SegformerForSemanticSegmentation
import torch.nn as nn

class CustomSegFormer(SegformerPreTrainedModel):
    def __init__(self, config):
        super().__init__(config)
        self.segformer = SegformerForSemanticSegmentation.from_pretrained("...")

    def forward(self, pixel_values, labels=None):
        # forward to get raw logits
        outputs = self.segformer(pixel_values)
        logits = outputs.logits
        
        # next, do whatever you want with the logits to compute loss
        loss = None
        if labels is not None:
               # upsample logits here
               # calculate custom loss here

        return output
```
author: NielsRogge

body: Not a vision engineer so probably not seeing every use case, but I'd favor minimal changes to the output of the model and to let users do what they wish with the output of the model. Resizing logits so that they can be fed to a custom loss of theirs doesn't look like it adds too much complexity and they'll already be computing the loss outside of the model.

body:
The regex actually takes everything preceding the last forward slash. It is the equivalent of the following:
```py
directory = "/".join(test_fname.split("/")[:-1])
```
I have tested it by raising in case it wasn't true for all files in the repository:

```py
    directory = re.findall(r"(.*)[/]", test_fname)[0]
    if directory != "/".join(test_fname.split("/")[:-1]):
        raise ValueError(directory, test_fname(
```

As this is confirmed, I am replacing it by the non-regex as it is easier to understand what it does.

body:
Overall this PR does seem to achieve the goal, that's great ! 

However I left a lot of NITs in code management. 
My biggest issue is with all the type checking everywhere in the code. I don't think there should be that many. Inputs should be checked when they come in from the user (  `generate`) but they shouldn't be checked over and over afterwards.
Keep a general input form, and use that. Everything will become simpler.

Edit: @patrickvonplaten rightfully made me realize that my current comments are not written in the nicest way. Please don't take this personally as I really didn't intend to demeanor the work done in this PR. Also, if you are unsure about what needs to be done, I would happily suggest the changes myself so you don't have to worry about these concerns. You could then review that we are purely making esthetic changes to the code.
author: Narsil

body:
The very next line calls `.cpu()` is it really needed here ?

Also the type hint seems wrong if the input can be a `torch.Tensor` and not just and `int`.

Not sure if the solution is to modify the type hint or make sure pure python objects are passed to this function.
author: Narsil

body:
The type of `current_seq` should be predetermined earlier IMO.
`tolist()` is actually most likely wrong, since lists are not hashable and so cannot index your trie dictionnaries.

Personnally I would vote that `current_seq` is forced to be a `List[int]` to prevent any `tolist()` creeping up (would have to be called before calling this function.).
author: Narsil

body: That type hint should be `Union[List[List[int]], torch.Tensor]` no ?
author: Narsil

body:
I don't think a function called `check_xxx` should ever have side effects, like changing class attributes.

If you want to do sanitation in that way, using a function `clean_xxx` that returns the cleaned objects and use
```python
def __init__(...):
     self.token_ids = self._clean_token_ids(token_ids)
```

is IMO much cleaner.
author: Narsil

body:
This code is extremely hard to read.
I think we can make it more readable.

If we want to check if there's a subset of  a sequence in another, we don't even need to do that `O(n²)` comparisons, just create the Trie, and raise and exception if a node is a not a pure leaf (if it has other children). That should I think cover what you are trying to do here. Checking that directly in the trie will be `O(n)`.
Is my assumption correct ?

Also this `is_int_list = isinstance(seq_k, List) and isinstance(seq_k[0], int)` is most likely incorrect since users can pass a list that is `[24, MyCustomObject()]`.

This should be fixed by doing the above cleanup and forcing the inputs to be consistent in the first place.

If the tensor is not empty, you add `.extend(` token_list with a list of tensors,
If the tensor is empty, you add it to the list ? (ending up with `[...., torch.Tensor([])]` )

If that's true, that seems very peculiar behavior.

I think what happened here, is that you modified the returned type of `Constraint.advance` to return a list of tokens, instead of a single token. Is that correct ?

In that case I think we should update all Constraints to return a list of token (most likely a single one) and use `token_list.extend(new_tokens)`.

If we don't control all constraints (users seem to be able to pass their own in `generate`) then we could have a check similar to yours, but we should explicitly call out that this is a backward compatibility thing.

Would you agree ?
author: Narsil

body:
This behavior depending on the type of the argument seems highly dangerous to me (dangerous in the sense that users won't understand what happens since we're changing the behavior in a non intuitive way).

I am not sure I understand disjunctive constraint, but if it's pick any one of the elements in my list as a `force_ids` then, I would suggest, that we change this to include only in the doc the fact that `List[List[List[int]]]]` And provide an example.

```
force_word_ids = [ 
  [ 
    [ 12, 24] # Miami
    [ 43 , 53] # Toronto
  ] ,
  [ [ 23] # Plane
  ]
]
# Then the result generation will contain exactly one of `Miami` or `Toronto`, and the word `Plane`
```

If we do that it becomes quite clear that having a single option of all `force_word_ids` will force all the words to be used, no ?

If I understand correctly and Disjunctinve is exactly a superset of the other one, then we can even just use it instead of the old `force_word_ids`. Performance might make us want to keep the old code though I am not sure how it works at this point.

Anyway, at this point we should definitely clean the inputs so that we have a consistent code later on. (Like just forcing everything to be `List[List[List[int]]]]`  even if we keep accepting the old format for backward compatibility.

body:
Since you said you enjoyed comments.

- This works perfectly fine
- I would use a `set` for `leaves` instead of a list to get O(1) lookups. (Optimizing early is not the purpose here, but using a datastructure that more closely matches how you use it is.)
- I think using `id(..)` is not super pythonistic.Your previous `-1` solution was actually elegant enough IMO.
- Design wise, I personally would create the trie first, and then check if `trie.has_subsets()` that would traverse the graph. It's slower but only by a constants, but it makes the check easy to disable (here you added the flag in the arguments but forgot to use it it seems). Also, we could make the error report all subsets at once, instead of letting user fix them one at a time.

Feel very free to ignore current code is OK,
author: Narsil

body:
Really picky NIT: `token_list.extend(advance)` is more pythonistic IMO.
It's also faster since it doesn't create a new  list.
It makes the understanding of what goes on easier IMO, because `.extend` only exists of iterable types, whereas without context `token_list` and `advance` could be read as `int` by an external reader
It also matches the `.append`  above.

Feel free to ignore

body:
I meant something like this:

```python
if forced_word_ids is not None:
    if constraints is None:
        constraints = []
    for word_ids in force_words_ids:
        is_seq_tensor = isinstance(word_ids[0], torch.LongTensor) and len(word_ids[0].size()) > 0
        if isinstance(word_ids[0], list) or is_seq_tensor:
            constraint = DisjunctiveConstraint(word_ids)
        else:
             constraint = PhrasalConstraint(word_ids)
         constraints.append(constraint)

# No change here
 is_constraint_gen_mode = constraints is not None
is_greedy_gen_mode = (num_beams == 1) and (num_beam_groups == 1) and do_sample is False and constraints is None
is_sample_gen_mode = (num_beams == 1) and (num_beam_groups == 1) and do_sample is True and constraints is None
is_beam_gen_mode = (num_beams > 1) and (num_beam_groups == 1) and do_sample is False and constraints is None

....

constrained_beam_scorer = ConstrainedBeamSearchScorer(
    constraints=constraints,
    ... 
)

```

I realized GH didn't make the suggestion on the whole block of code since it's separate changes for it, that's why you couldn't get what I was suggesting.

Basically the idea is to get `constraints` to be treated as a single thing everywhere it's possible.
Since `force_word_ids` is creating a new constraint, well, we sanitize and create the objects early on, and then everything is simpler later on.

FYI the same strategy is applied to `logits_processor` and `stopping_criteria`.

This `generate` function is definitely quite complex and managing its complexity is definitely not easy while we're trying to expand its capabilities (which is a good thing ! )
author: Narsil

body: Thanks for clarifying for me !
author: Narsil

body:
Right, I understand now. 

Sort of why I went with this direction was because I wanted to ***visibly separate*** the constrained beam search part of the huge generate function code. 

It's actually necessary than a design choice for `logits_processor` to be defined before branching out to the different generation modes since *all* generation modes use them, but constraints can only be used in `constrained_beam_search` mode and can't integrate into any other modes. 

Considering that the type checking and processing on the `constraints` & `forced_words_ids `is complex and not short with 20+ lines, I thought it'd be actually cleaner to deal with it under
```python
if is_constrained_mode:
    ... # actually do the type checks & other processing, since it can only be used here
    # kind of a designated sanctuary for all constraints-related logic, separate from the rest of 
    # the complexities of generate()
```

Here, `(batch_size, num_heads, patch_size, sequence_length)` is not correct. For ViT for instance, the sequence length is equal to (config.image_size // config.patch_size)**2 + 1. So the `attentions` have shape (`batch_size, num_heads, sequence_length, sequence_length)`.

For Swin Transformer, this will be different (not sure what the sequence length is there). Swin has a different sequence length per layer.

body:
```suggestion
*Non-autoregressive text to speech (TTS) models such as FastSpeech (Ren et al., 2019) can synthesize speech significantly faster than previous autoregressive mod- els with comparable quality. The training of FastSpeech model relies on an au- toregressive teacher model for duration prediction (to provide more information as input) and knowledge distillation (to simplify the data distribution in out- put), which can ease the one-to-many mapping problem (i.e., multiple speech variations correspond to the same text) in TTS. However, FastSpeech has sev- eral disadvantages: 1) the teacher-student distillation pipeline is complicated and time-consuming, 2) the duration extracted from the teacher model is not accu- rate enough, and the target mel-spectrograms distilled from teacher model suf- fer from information loss due to data simplification, both of which limit the voice quality. In this paper, we propose FastSpeech 2, which addresses the is- sues in FastSpeech and better solves the one-to-many mapping problem in TTS by 1) directly training the model with ground-truth target instead of the simpli- fied output from teacher, and 2) introducing more variation information of speech (e.g., pitch, energy and more accurate duration) as conditional inputs. Specifi- cally, we extract duration, pitch and energy from speech waveform and directly take them as conditional inputs in training and use predicted values in inference. We further design FastSpeech 2s, which is the first attempt to directly generate speech waveform from text in parallel, enjoying the benefit of fully end-to-end inference. Experimental results show that 1) FastSpeech 2 achieves a 3x train- ing speed-up over FastSpeech, and FastSpeech 2s enjoys even faster inference speed; 2) FastSpeech 2 and 2s outperform FastSpeech in voice quality, and Fast- Speech 2 can even surpass autoregressive models. Audio samples are available at https://speechresearch.github.io/fastspeech2/.*
```

body:
Hey @patrickvonplaten, thanks for the feedback. I added HiFi-GAN in the lastest commits. Some questions:

1. **HiFi-GAN Trainability.** Should HiFi-GAN (or `FastSpeech2ForWaveformGeneration`) be trainable? HiFi-GAN is trained just like any normal model, but during inference, the user should call `remove_weight_norm`. For now, I just manually called the function in the `FastSpeech2ForWaveformGeneration.forward()` function, but this might mean that the class is not trainable. Alternatively, I could check if the model is in `eval` mode and only call `remove_weight_norm` if it's in `model.train` is `False`. Do you have a preference here?
2. **HiFi-GAN Configuration.** I hard-coded all HiFi-GAN parameters instead of polluting `FastSpeech2Config`. HiFi-GAN does come in 3 variations (check `config_v*.jsons` [here](https://github.com/jik876/hifi-gan)), but the V1 model has the most amount of checkpoints, covering LJ Speech, VCTK, and a "universal" version for general-purpose decoding. The hard-coded parameters thus follow the V1 configuration.
3. **Waveform Output.** Because input tokens are padded, the output mel-spectrogram will also be of different lengths (although they will appear to be of equal lengths due to batching). When we pass this batch of padded mel-spectrograms to HiFi-GAN, the vocoder will also produce padded audio (again, due to batching, the waveforms may appear to be of equal length, but the shorter samples will contain meaningless noise at the end). I'm trying to think of a good UI/UX to deal with this. Any input would be greatly appreciated!

I realize this PR has been WIP for a very long time. Apologies for the delay, and thank you so much for the review!

body:
This is required for testing Roberta with the past inputs.

Originally, the `input_ids` is generated with `ids_tensor()`, which might contain `1`, i.e. `TFRobertaEmbeddings.padding_idx`.

Considering a simplified example: `input_ids = [7, 1, 9, 1, 4]`, and let it be the initial `present` inputs.
The `position_ids` computed in `TFRobertaEmbeddings.create_position_ids_from_input_ids` would be:

```
mask:
    [1, 0, 1, 0, 1]

cum_sum:
    [1, 1, 2, 2, 3]

incremental_indices:
    [1, 0, 2, 0, 3]

position_ids:
    [2, 1, 3, 1, 4]
```

Now let's add a next token `5` as the new `present`, and `[7, 1, 9, 1, 4]` as `past` with `past_key_values_length=5` (which counts the 2 padding idx).

The position id for the next token `5` would be
```
mask:
    [1]

cum_sum:
    [1]

incremental_indices:
    [6] (with `past_key_values_length=5`)

position_ids:
    [7] --> the `next_token` has  `position_id 7` 
```

However, if we pass the concatenated input `[7, 1, 9, 1, 4, 5]` to the model without past `past_key_values_length`,
```
mask:
    [1, 0, 1, 0, 1, 1]

cum_sum:
    [1, 1, 2, 2, 3, 4]

incremental_indices:
    [1, 0, 2, 0, 3, 4]

position_ids:
    [2, 1, 3, 1, 4, 5] --> the `next_token` has  `position_id 5` 
```

**Summary**

This will fail the testing for `past` when `padding_idx` appears in the initial `input_ids`.

body:
If I understood correctly, the initial problem was that the special token was not found in the `convert_tokens_to_string` output while given as individual tokens in the input. So, I think we should refocus the test on this check by testing the method on all the special tokens of each spm tokenizer - which are stored in the `all_special_tokens` attribute.

So I suggest the following changes:
```suggestion
        special_tokens =  tokenizer.all_special_tokens
        special_tokens_string = tokenizer.convert_tokens_to_string(special_tokens)
        for special_token in special_tokens:
            self.assertIn(special_token, special_tokens_string)
```

These changes cause the test to fail now for `ReformerTokenizer`, `BigBirdTokenizer` and `BertGenerationTokenizer` which are also spm tokenizers. 

Don't hesitate to tell me if you ever wrote the previous test with another case in mind! :smile:
author: SaulLu

```suggestion
        if self.test_rust_tokenizer:

            special_tokens_string_rust = rust_tokenizer.convert_tokens_to_string(special_tokens)
```

body:
Okay, after adding this test, a few previously passing tests failed. This is because of the space you mentioned in another comment. I removed the space, so now the code looks like this:

```python
def convert_tokens_to_string(self, tokens):
    ...
    out_string += self.sp_model.decode(current_sub_tokens) + token  # << a space used to be here
    ...
```

And now all the tests pass, including `T5Tokenizer`.
I will note that I changed calls to `self.sp_model.decode_pieces` to `self.sp_model.decode`, as `decode`, if needed, delegates to `decode_pieces`, and this makes the code uniform, in my opinion.

body:
I have written the following test:
```python
def test_sentencepiece_tokenize_and_decode(self):
    if not self.test_sentencepiece:
        return

    text = "This is text to test the tokenizer."
    if self.test_rust_tokenizer:
        tokenizer = self.get_tokenizer()
        rust_tokenizer = self.get_rust_tokenizer()

        slow_ids = tokenizer(text).input_ids
        fast_ids = rust_tokenizer(text).input_ids
        self.assertEqual(slow_ids, fast_ids)

        slow_decoded = tokenizer.decode(slow_ids)
        fast_decoded = rust_tokenizer.decode(slow_ids)
        self.assertEqual(slow_decoded, fast_decoded)
```

The behaviors of slow and fast tokenizers in `decode` are indeed mismatched, and the mismatch is, to me, quite odd. Let's take a look at a few failures (these tokenizers pass `test_sentencepiece_tokenize_and_convert_tokens_to_string`):

|Tokenizer|Output|
|-|-|
|Albert|`'[CLS]this is text to test the tokenizer.[SEP]' != '[CLS] this is text to test the tokenizer.[SEP]'`|
|Barthez|`'<s>This is text to test the tokenizer.</s>' != '<s> This is text to test the tokenizer.</s>'`|
|BigBird|`'[CLS] This is text to test the tokenizer. [SEP]' != '[CLS] This is text to test the tokenizer.[SEP]'`|
|Camembert|`'<s>This is text to test the tokenizer.</s>' != '<s> This is text to test the tokenizer.</s>'`|
|MBart50|`'en_XXThis is text to test the tokenizer.</s>' != 'en_XX This is text to test the tokenizer.</s>'`|

It would seem that the correct behavior, as dictated by the fast tokenizers, is that the BOS token should have a space after it, but the EOS token comes right after the last token without a space.

This seems like a small thing to fix, but the question is whether the problem is that space, or is it something bigger that I'm not aware of.

body:
I'm really sorry I didn't get back to you sooner, honestly it's a bit hard to know which is the best choice to make.

There are 2 important things to know that we didn't necessarily mention before, added tokens can define additional arguments (`lstrip`, `rstrip`, `normalized`, etc) that can only be taken into account by fast tokenizers and fast tokenizers sometimes have a design that is particularly lossy for added tokens (see [this issue](https://github.com/huggingface/transformers/issues/16334)) which was a kind of inattended behavior. These 2 elements are important to say that the output of the fast tokenizer is not necessarily the source of truth.

I promise, tomorrow I'll take an hour to clone your branch and play with it to get an idea of the trade off that would be best for me (after that I'll probably need the opinion of a core maintainer of transformers). To tell you the truth, the trade off that is bothering me is between 1) minimizing the differences between slow and fast, 2) minimizing the complexity of the code - in short, having the most logical behavior for the users. 

Don't hesitate to share if you have also thought about this question and have an opinion to share! Sorry again for not being enough helpful
author: SaulLu

body:
I think we should split the decision in two:
1. Tokenizers that don't have a fast version, and then there's nothing to compare to, so users using `AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained()` even with `use_fast=True` will get a slow tokenizer
2. Tokenizers that have both versions. This part, I think, will require some investigation from HF to see whether there are use-cases of a slow tokenizer when a fast one is available. As a user, I do `pip install transformers` and get `tokenizers` as a dependency. Then `AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained()` by default gives a fast tokenizer, if one's available. Why would I want to use a slow tokenizer? Answering this question will motivate the decision on what to do with the slow tokenizers' code. It could be that the code can be ugly-but-working since it won't get touched too often, but if the fast tokenizers get changed frequently and we want to keep feature parity with the slow tokenizers, then maintainable code is probably a priority.

All the best, Lucile 🙂
author: beneyal

body:
Hi @beneyal ,

As promised I've spent some time today diving back into your PR. In the end, I think that what you have proposed is a good solution given all the constraints that we have.

The only thing I can think of is that we can probably afford to do the same thing with tokenizers that don't have a fast version (yet). I'm thinking of the case of derberta_v2 which has had a fast version in the meantime, if we had merged this PR before adding the fast tokenizer the new tests would have failed and we wouldn't have had the possibility to fix it on the fast side.

>  Why would I want to use a slow tokenizer?

To come back to one of the last questions you raised, we have slow versions for several reasons, including the fact that they are extremely close to the original code of the authors (this is the source of truth for building the fast tokenizer - however this source of truth disappears when some features have not been implemented in the original code, which is very often the case with added tokens), they are python versions so they are more readable by users and for backward compatibility reasons (e.g. there are options in the `decode` method of the slow tokenizers that are not available in the fasts). 

Before I do a new PR review, do you think you'll have time to resolve the merge conflicts? If you don't have the time, don't hesitate to tell me, I can take care of it and open a PR on your branch with the necessary changes (as you already gave a lot of time to this PR and the merge conflicts are partly my fault ). :smile: 

Thank you again for your patience and for having worked so hard on the code base!

body:
I see! Can you try to change this line (in `test_tokenization_deberta_v2.py`) :

https://github.com/huggingface/transformers/blob/dced262409177586bb510b6b724c762fb89da0e8/tests/deberta_v2/test_tokenization_deberta_v2.py#L42

by `tokenizer = DebertaV2Tokenizer(SAMPLE_VOCAB, unk_token="<unk>")`.

I think it's because the `SAMPLE_VOCAB` defines the unknown token as `"<unk>"` and not as `"[UNK]"` which is the default value for `DebertaV2Tokenizer`.

EDIT: I've inspected the content inside and it's probably even better to replace it by 
```python
tokenizer = DebertaV2Tokenizer(
    SAMPLE_VOCAB, 
        bos_token="[CLS]",
        eos_token="[SEP]",
        unk_token="<unk>",
        sep_token="[SEP]",
        pad_token="<pad>",
        cls_token="[CLS]",
        mask_token="[MASK]",
)
```
as it's how the special tokens are defined inside `SAMPLE_VOCAB`

body:
Overall, I'm in favor of adding the new Marian checkpoints the way it is shown here. The change from having a Marian model that always **force-tied** encoder and decoder embeddings to a Marian model that can switch between **force-tied** and no-tied encoder input embeddings and encoder output embeddings is the better option here IMO even though it does go a bit again our philosophy of not changing existing model code. 

The main reasons why I'm in favor of the approach as it's implemented now are (with the feedback given below):
- All the changes of this PR are also applicable to existing Marian V1 checkpoints. More specifically all Marian V1 checkpoints can be loaded here with `share_encoder_decoder_embeddings=False` and then fine-tuned with embeddings not being tied.
- Marian V2 comes from the exact same library as Marian V1 and is the same model. Creating a new name here (Marian V2) could confuse users.

Thoughts @LysandreJik @sgugger ?
author: patrickvonplaten

body:
As discussed offline, I think we should rewrite this a bit. Even if `config.share_encoder_decoder_embeddings is False` we should clone those weights to initialized encoder and decoder with it. This makes this PR much more appealing for existing Marian models. E.g. one could then simple load a Marian model with:


```python
from transformers import MarianMTModel

model = MarianMTModel.from_pretrained("Helsinki-NLP/opus-mt-en-de", share_encoder_decoder_embeddings=False)

# now one can fine-tune the model with encoder / decoder embeddings not being shared which has been shown to lead to improved results
```

body:
No there was a bug:

> Have to change the tokenization word state machine. With chunking we can have 2 chunks
being concatenated, both containing a separate space. Hopefully this code is readable enough.

The core thing is that the end `tokens` I could present the `decode` function would be

[ ....., 0, 0, 0, 4, 4, 4, 0, 0, 0]  + [ 0, 0, 4, 0, 0, 0]

That created `char_offsets`  with 2 consecutive {char: " ", ...}, (that's ok and really what are in the tokens). and the current code for words would output *twice* the previously seen word (here `{"EXISTS, ..}, {"EXIST, ...}` since it assumed that a space meant "new word" all the time.

Making the modification as a full rewrite makes the final code simpler IMO.

body:
Not it's two things.

1- The cutoff instead of filing the buffer, changed that here instead of using the middle value of the waveform we instead don't use anything. `tokenizer.decode([])` essentially. I think this is more correct. There's a test that should ensure that striding more than the length of the audio should trigger an error. We could trigger if the stride leaves the tokens empty and change the test slightly to get back the "B" instead (like `strde = (4999, 5000)`).

2- The `rescale_stride` changed and creates slight changes. It used to do a pure `inputs.shape[1] / logits.shape[1]` instead of using `model.config.inputs_to_logits_ratio`. It means that the rescaling of the stride was slightly different than now because the convolutions sides are not taken into consideration anymore. I think it's more correct now than before (because of my testing, but I am not sure it's 100% correct).
Since this is a random model it is highly susceptible to this change and that just shifted the offsets by one meaning the "B" was moved from the second test into the first one.

The main thing is that we keep "OB XB" is the start of the full random string tested above on the full audio without strides.

This looks good! I don't have much to add, except that I think the PR could be greatly simplified by:
- It seems like most of the changes to GPT-2 could simply be achieved by having the positional word embeddings be full of zeros so that the addition does nothing.
- A lot of changes are linked to your `black` probably not seeing the line character limit is 119. You should run `make fixup` from the root of your clone after installing the dependencies with `pip install -e .[quality]`
- There's a `datasets` submodule that was added and that is not necessary 

Finally, I'm not sure the default example applies here; if possible a full working example would go a long way towards understanding how the model behaves. If you think this is better suited in a notebook, this is definitely fine too.

Pinging @patrickvonplaten and @sgugger for knowledge; no need to do a review just yet, but if you have any other ideas regarding the first RL model in the library, feel free to mention them!

body:
Thanks for the model addition. I've left a few nits here and there (in particular we should respect the name selected by the authors, which is Decision Transformers with a space).

My main concern is around the modeling file code. The core principle of the library is that each modeling file should be independent from the others, and we have designed mechanisms to make sure copy-pasted code stays in sync across files. We make an exception to that rule for models that works for several blocks (like encoder-decoder models, or models that can accept several architectures as a "backbone") but it doesn't seem to be the case here, so I'm curious why you're not pushing to include the code of GPT-2 @patrickvonplaten @LysandreJik

body:
Agree here with @sgugger I think. Sorry @edbeeching I might have been a bit misleading in my messages on Slack. 

To be more precise, I think we should never import a specfic model like `GPT2Model` into another modeling file. I think there are two possibilities here:

1. Decision Transformers only works with GPT2 (all it's checkpoints are GPT2-like checkpoints). In this case I 100% agree with @sgugger and we should just copy-paste the whole GPT2Model architecture under the name `DecisionTransformerModel` into this file and add a nice `# Copied from ...` line to ensure the code is kept up to date.
2. If we have checkpoints for GPT2, GPT-J and GPT-Neo, then I can be convinced into importing the `AutoModelForCausalLM` class here and make the model a composition model.

However given that the model is just a thin wrapper above GPT2 (e.g. essentially just uses GPT2 as the encoder), I also prefer 1. here.


Sorry, @edbeeching I might have been misleading on Slack (first time I'm doing a full review on the PR)
author: patrickvonplaten

```suggestion
Instead of streaming the dataset from the hub you can also stream it from disk. This can be helpful for long training runs where the connection can be interrupted sometimes. To stream locally you simply need to clone the datasets and replace the dataset name with their path. In this example we store the data in a folder called `data`: 
```

```suggestion
mkdir data
git -C "./data" clone https://huggingface.co/datasets/lvwerra/codeparrot-clean-train

```

body:
Not sure what's the best approach, but testing against real values seems better if you can easily craft one.

Like maybe we can craft (1, 2, 2) tensors that simulate a model output on 2x2 images and make sure everything including the masks. (WIthtout even ever loading the model, we're testing the feature extractor here is what I am implying)

Something else if crafting the values is too tedious/brittle, in the pipeline I use  nested_simplify to enable doing that:

```python
self.assertEqual(panoptic_segmentation, [{"segmentation": [[1, 2,3 ]], "segments": [{"id":1, "category_id": ...}, {...}, ....]},
 {"segmentation": [[1, 2,3 ]], "segments": [{"id":1, "category_id": ...}, {...}, ....]}])
```

It currently better than no test. And if the testing is hard to make more complete, we don't necessarily have to.
Tests are relevant as long as they are readable IMO, so a complex tests looses value.
This one is simple and that's good.

Not sure if this applies here and so forth, but I think the test could check a little more than the outer length format IMO, is what I was trying to convey

body:
Use `-1` because the `0` in `global_attention_mask` is used to indicate `local attention` rather than `not to attend`:

https://github.com/huggingface/transformers/blob/c1000e703b84b4e0f57e204e711e184379f528be/src/transformers/models/led/modeling_led.py#L1536

`global_attention_mask` will be used as follows

https://github.com/huggingface/transformers/blob/c1000e703b84b4e0f57e204e711e184379f528be/src/transformers/models/led/modeling_led.py#L1643-L1653

therefore, `-1` will gvie `0` here for the (merged) `attention_mask`, which makes sense.

body:
Awesome work! Code looks much cleaner now and the `prepare_inputs_for_generation_...` functions are greatly simplified. 

Left 1,2 nits. Finally, it would be nice if you could also update the TF encoder-decoder model templates (copy of TFBart) so that this test doesn't fail. 

The fastest way to test these things locally is to do the following:
- 1. Update the templates similar to how TFBart was updated. Commit your changes
- 2. Create a new TFBart-like model with the `add-new-model` command & run tests for the created model.
- 3. run `git reset --hard`` so that the new model code disappears again
- 4. If tests are all passing, then you can commit, if not repeat 1-4

Would be nice if @Rocketknight1 could also take a look here

body:
I will approve since `thing` and `stuff` seem to be standard in that case.

A more descriptive name seems more suitable from a pure code standpoint so I'll let you decide @FrancescoSaverioZuppichini 

What I had in mind was more along the lines of:

`ids_to_fuse: Set[int], *optional*` : If the id is in the set, then only 1 instance of them can be outputted, for instance the class `sky` could not be part of 2 separate instances. If no map is passed, no fusion occurs.

Other name options that come to mind:
- `class_ids_to_fuse`
- `merge` might be better than `fuse`.
- `single_instance_classes/ids`
- `singleton_ids`

No need to use a Dict imo, since the default is not fusing, not belonging to the dict would mean not fusing, and so the dict values become redundant.
And the name really describes how it is used.

body:
Forcing `label_ids_to_fuse` early on to be a `set` (maybe empty) is IMO better, since you can start relying on the variable type instead of having `if ... None` everywhere.

```python
  if label_ids_to_fuse is None:
            logger.warning("`label_ids_to_fuse` unset. No instance will be fused.")
            label_ids_to_fuse = set()
```
And then here.:
```python
should_fuse = pred_class in label_ids_to_fuse
``` 
No check necessary, you already changed `label_ids_to_fuse` to be a set.

Again the only reason why you don't use `my_function(....label_ids_to_fuse=set())` is because `set` is mutable.
With immutable types (like `tuple`) it would be preferable to use a default argument imo.

> Note: Technically you can use `frozenset` as the default argument but my personal take is that keeping things simple with `None` as default argument is easy enough and understandable too `frozenset` is not that widely used. 
To be fair, `set` itself is probably not used that much either, but it does have a generator comprehension at least `myset = {letter for letter in "hello"}`)

body:
I think replacing the first paragraph with the suggestion below makes it easier for the user to map each `generate` to its `GenerationMixin` class :)

Each framework has a `generate` method for auto-regressive text generation implemented in their respective `GenerationMixin` class:

- PyTorch [`~generation_utils.GenerationMixin.generate`] is implemented in [`~generation_utils.GenerationMixin`].
- TensorFlow [`~generation_tf_utils.TFGenerationMixin.generate`] is implemented in [`~generation_tf_utils.TFGenerationMixin`].
- Flax/JAX [`~generation_flax_utils.FlaxGenerationMixin.generate`] is implemented in [`~generation_flax_utils.FlaxGenerationMixin`].
author: stevhliu

Thanks for working on it, it looks like a very cool model. There some naming convention that must be applied

- `***Block/s` -> `***Layer/s`
- use `Stage` when we stack multiple `***Layers` together
- when possible use `hidden_size/s` instead of `channels`

is hard to understand with the indices what is happening, maybe a refactor?(check my code)

```suggestion
def forward(self, hidden_states):
    # reversing the hidden_states, we start from the last
    hidden_states = hidden_states[::-1]
    features_fused = []
    # first layer only used the last hidden_state
    feature_fused = self.layers[0](hidden_states[0])
    # looping from the last layer to the second
    for hidden_state, layer in zip(hidden_states[1:], self.layers[1:]):
        feature_fused = layer(feature_fused, hidden_state)
        features_fused.append(feature_fused)
   return features_fused
```
author: FrancescoSaverioZuppichini

author: sgugger

body:
```suggestion
*While originally designed for natural language processing tasks, the self-attention mechanism has recently taken various computer vision areas by storm. However, the 2D nature of images brings three challenges for applying self-attention in computer vision. (1) Treating images as 1D sequences neglects their 2D structures. (2) The quadratic complexity is too expensive for high-resolution images. (3) It only captures spatial adaptability but ignores channel adaptability. In this paper, we propose a novel large kernel attention (LKA) module to enable self-adaptive and long-range correlations in self-attention while avoiding the above issues. We further introduce a novel neural network based on LKA, namely Visual Attention Network (VAN). While extremely simple, VAN outperforms the state-of-the-art vision transformers and convolutional neural networks with a large margin in extensive experiments, including image classification, object detection, semantic segmentation, instance segmentation, etc. Code is available at [this https URL](https://github.com/Visual-Attention-Network/VAN-Classification).*
```

body:
```suggestion
El uso de un modelo pre-entrenado tiene importantes ventajas. Reduce los costos de computación, la huella de carbono, y te permite utilizar modelos de última generación sin tener que entrenar uno desde cero. 🤗 Transformers proporciona acceso a miles de modelos pre-entrenados en una amplia gama de tareas. Cuando utilizas un modelo pre-entrenado, lo entrenas con un dataset específico para tu tarea. Esto se conoce como fine-tuning, una técnica de entrenamiento increíblemente poderosa. En este tutorial haremos fine-tuning a un modelo pre-entrenado con un framework de Deep Learning de tu elección:
```
author: omarespejel

body:

body:
Hi,

Sorry after looking into the code, it doesn't seem like this is mergeable. The reason is that the `PerceiverAudioPreprocessor` class is used in the `PerceiverMultimodalPreprocessor` class. The latter takes a bunch of modality-specific preprocessors as input, and passes `pos=pos `to each of those preprocessors. However, if we drop the "pos" for the audio preprocessor, but keep it for the others, the code will fail.

So to make it work, one should remove the pos argument from all preprocessors; meaning `PerceiverImagePreprocessor`, `PerceiverOneHotPreprocessor` and `PerceiverAudioPreprocessor`.

Next, in the `PerceiverMultimodalPreprocessor` class, passing the `pos` argument can be dropped.
author: NielsRogge

```suggestion
Los modelos son [`torch.nn.Module`](https://pytorch.org/docs/stable/nn.html#torch.nn.Module) o [`tf.keras.Model`](https://www.tensorflow.org/api_docs/python/tf/keras/Model) estándares así que podrás usarlos en tu training loop usual. Sin embargo, para facilitar las cosas, 🤗 Transformers provee una clase [`Trainer`] para PyTorch que añade funcionalidades para entrenamiento distribuido, precición mixta, y más. ```suggestion
```
```suggestion
Como con el [`pipeline`], el tokenizador aceptará una lista de inputs. Además, el tokenizador también puede rellenar (pad, en inglés) y truncar el texto para devolver un lote (batch, en inglés) de longitud uniforme:
```
author: omarespejel

body:
```suggestion
Después, el tokenizador convierte los tokens a números para construir un tensor que servirá como input para el modelo. Esto es conocido como el *vocabulario* del modelo.
```
author: omarespejel

body:
```suggestion
Un tokenizador es responsable de procesar el texto a un formato que sea entendible para el modelo. Primero, el tokenizador separará el texto en palabras llamadas *tokens*. Hay múltiples reglas que gobiernan el proceso de tokenización incluyendo el cómo separar una palabra y en qué nivel (aprende más sobre tokenización [aquí](./tokenizer_summary)). Lo más importante es recordar que necesitarás instanciar el tokenizador con el mismo nombre del modelo para asegurar que estás usando las mismas reglas de tokenización con las que el modelo fue preentrenado.
```
author: omarespejel

Finalmente, después de que has ajustado tu modelo preentrenado, ¡por favor considera compartirlo (ve el tutorial [aquí](./model_sharing)) con la comunidad en el Model Hub para democratizar el NLP! 🤗

body:
Hey, this looks really good! I have a couple of nitpicks before we can merge it, though:

1) `TFXLMMainLayer.call()` is missing an output type.
2) Some of the arguments (e.g. `cache`, `start_positions`, `end_positions`) are annotated as `bool` when they're not - `cache` should be `Dict[str, tf.Tensor]` and `start_positions` and `end_positions` are `Union[np.ndarray, tf.Tensor]`. The class docs often have type information you can use, check them out [here](https://huggingface.co/docs/transformers/model_doc/xlm)!

I've left a few comments on the PR but I'm extremely nervous about changing a core piece of the library like this. This function is not intended to be performant as the conversion should be done once and for all, and then the TF weights should be uploaded in the repo for every other users.

As such, I'd rather keep a version I know works than do some refactor and changes in the code.

This is a personal addition to prevent bad reshapes from ever happening (Like `zeros((2, 3)).reshape((3, 2))` which would be a logic error.

The follow up PR, completely removes the need for this code (no random squeeze/unsqueeze either)
author: Narsil

body:
I think it is still useful (in this PR, not the follow up), since if dimensions don't match in the end we will crash here instead of later when attempting to write the data to the weight in the model.

In the follow up, `apply_transpose` don't have the reference to the tensor anymore so this check cannot exist (but operation is easily invertible)

body:
> This is a personal addition to prevent bad reshapes from ever happening (Like zeros((2, 3)).reshape((3, 2)) which would be a logic error.

I don't think it ever happened in the tests at least but it felt like blindly reshaping could cause issues.
author: Narsil

body: Silently ignoring weights is absolutely not the right move, having spent several hours debugging things like that. And saying "it's going to be fixed in that other PR" for functionality at core is not an answer either.
author: sgugger

body:
> Silently ignoring weights is absolutely not the right move

This is exactly what this prevents. It prevents silent errors (logic errors) and forces them to be real errors. 
The final `assert` of this function does the real erroring (Regardless of the attempt of reshaping) (I know we moved from `assert` to `if ... raise ValueError` I kept it to be closer to the original, but happy to make it `if raise ValueError`.

> "it's going to be fixed in that other PR" for functionality at core is not an answer either.

I don't think I claimed other PR solves this, I said it does not require the code anymore, meaning there's a single point of failure if tensors don't match. What I actually meant, is maybe we can follow the same path (so taking part of the other PR, and make it this one's code).

I can actually follow the same route, and make `apply_transpose` only about tensor reshaping and make validation another step as they are 2 distinct responsibility.

I agree with Sylvain that splitting in two PRs would make reviews easier, and would remove some of the stress that comes from a PR touching the core serialization code.

> Would a solution like using the new code in a try.. except and running the exact old code if it fails make everyone feel less nervous ? (I know it would reduce my own nervousness)

body:
Not super keen on having such a core part of the library rely on the private attribute `h5py._hl`. From a quick look at the `h5py` repo, it seems like it was introduced in version 2.0. 

Do we have guarantees that it will remain? Is it the same as `h5py.Dataset`/is there a public-facing API that can be used for it?
author: LysandreJik

body: Good catch, I looked up the concrete class , `h5py.Dataset` is better (it's an alias for `_hl.dataset.Dataset`)

author: sgugger

body:
pinging @sgugger to sync on this new feature.

Since it's possible that state_dict may contain keys that aren't in `loaded_state_dict_keys` I think the logic probably needs to be:

```
for k in loaded_state_dict_keys:
    if k not in state_dict.keys():
        continue
```

At this moment though there should be an exact match, since the current code is:

```
            if low_cpu_mem_usage:
                # save the keys
                if is_sharded:
                    loaded_state_dict_keys = sharded_metadata["all_checkpoint_keys"]
                else:
                    state_dict = load_state_dict(resolved_archive_file)
                    loaded_state_dict_keys = [k for k in state_dict.keys()]
                    del state_dict  # free CPU memory - will reload again later
```
but I'm concerned if in the future it might not match, so proposing a more future-proof code.
author: stas00

body: and it sounds like we are missing some tests.
author: stas00

body: If this is not addressed here, loading a big model in 1x memory consumption from chucked checkpoints is not possible. I think my approach is safer and will fail in the cases that are supposed to fail, otherwise, we are silently skipping keys that may be supposed to be there. Let me know your thoughts.
author: FrancescoSaverioZuppichini

body:
As said before:
> Any fix for low memory loading and sharded checkpoints would need to be in its own PR with a test.

PRs should be focused on one task: adding a new model is different from fixing a bug. :-)
author: sgugger

body:
>otherwise, we are silently skipping keys that may be supposed to be there. Let me know your thoughts.

That was exactly my point in reverse. What if we need to skip some keys? 

My assumption is that `loaded_state_dict_keys` is 100% correct because the model was initialized using those keys. So it's the master list of what we expect to load.

and if we are missing some keys, we should have sorted it out earlier when we populated `loaded_state_dict_keys`

body:
this is a trickier change -- in the original version, `attn_mask` is a string that is then used in the debug message

To preserve the debug message, the new version should be something like:
```python
for attn_name, attn_mask in [("head_mask", head_mask), ("cross_attn_head_mask", cross_attn_head_mask)]:
    if attn_mask is not None and tf.executing_eagerly():
        tf.debugging.assert_equal(
            shape_list(attn_mask)[0],
            len(self.layers),
            message=f"The {attn_name} should be specified for {len(self.layers)} layers, but it is for {shape_list(attn_mask)[0]}.",
        )
```

```suggestion
El comando de arriba instala la versión `master` más actual en vez de la última versión estable. La versión `master` es útil para obtener los últimos avances de  🤗 Transformers. Por ejemplo, se puede dar el caso de que un error fue corregido después de la última versión estable pero aún no se ha liberado un nuevo lanzamiento. Sin embargo, existe la posibilidad de que la versión `master` no sea estable. El equipo trata de mantener la versión `master` operacional y la mayoría de los errores son resueltos en unas cuantas horas o un día. Si encuentras algún problema, por favor abre un [Issue](https://github.com/huggingface/transformers/issues) para que pueda ser corregido más rápido.
```

author: patrickvonplaten

body:
`TFBert` and `TFEncoderDecoder` were both updated ([PR](https://github.com/huggingface/transformers/pull/15944/files#diff-78a88286ed61e08098e9c0f2a9e75b0aea93d9b908d8e5da4b9b023bc27fc3e7)). The [test](https://github.com/huggingface/transformers/blob/b7fa1e3deebc3917965ad673e9fb61301416078c/tests/encoder_decoder/test_modeling_tf_encoder_decoder.py#L601) in question also doesn't have the `num_beams` nor the `sample` arguments in generate, meaning that it should be going through the updated `greedy_search`, which has the updated `past` format 🤔 

@ydshieh, can I ask you to confirm that `generate()` calls the new `_generate()` internally ([this one](https://github.com/huggingface/transformers/blob/master/src/transformers/generation_tf_utils.py#L565))? If not, it means that it is going through the old code, which may have some `past`-related issue (I made some ad hoc changes to comply with the new `past` format, but they may be incomplete.
author: gante

body: I can check the logic flow, will report back.
author: ydshieh

body:
- calls the new _generate() -> Yes
- then `return self.greedy_search` [here](https://github.com/huggingface/transformers/blob/a6271967c971cab5d882bba87c19406f30cdc7e4/src/transformers/generation_tf_utils.py#L1540)
- This line [model_inputs = self.prepare_inputs_for_generation](https://github.com/huggingface/transformers/blob/a6271967c971cab5d882bba87c19406f30cdc7e4/src/transformers/generation_tf_utils.py#L1920) gives `past_key_values`:
  - 1st step: None
  - 2nd step: a tuple of 2 elements (which shouldn't be)

It is probably this method [TFEncoderDecoderModel.prepare_inputs_for_generation](https://github.com/huggingface/transformers/blob/a6271967c971cab5d882bba87c19406f30cdc7e4/src/transformers/models/encoder_decoder/modeling_tf_encoder_decoder.py#L694) should be corrected, rather than in `TFBertModel`.

Considering it was me who worked on `TFEncoderDecoderModel`, I can fix it instead.
@gante, WDYT? If yes, do you have some hints/tips for me about the changes on the format you have done?
